text: The video shows an indirect inguinal hernia on the left side, with a significant amount of omentum protruding into the hernia sac.
The surgeon begins the procedure by reducing the hernia contents, gently manipulating the omentum and other tissues back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper to carefully manipulate and position the hernia contents, with assistance from a second person.
The surgeon carefully pulls down the omentum that is protruding into the hernia defect, using gentle traction to reduce the herniated tissue.
The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper to manipulate the hernia contents, with the option to use a Maryland dissector for precise handling.
The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper to carefully reduce the hernia contents, avoiding the sharp tip of the Harmonic device.
The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper to carefully extract a large amount of omentum from the hernia defect, ensuring gentle manipulation to avoid damage.
The assistant uses an atraumatic grasper to gently push the omental content from above, facilitating its reduction through the hernia defect.
The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon, adjusting its position within the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to place a mesh with a six-centimeter margin around the healthy tissue surrounding the hernia defect.
The surgeon prepares a 15 by 15 cm mesh for placement over the hernia defect, ensuring it covers the area adequately.
The surgeon positions a mesh to cover six centimeters around the margin of the hernia defect, ensuring adequate coverage of the surrounding healthy tissue.
The surgeon positions the center of the mesh directly over the center of the hernia defect, ensuring proper alignment for coverage.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the sigmoid colon and prepares to introduce a 15x15 cm mesh over the hernia defect, ensuring it covers six centimeters around the healthy margin.
The surgeon positions the mesh so that its center aligns precisely with the center of the hernia defect, ensuring even coverage.
The surgeon begins firing the tacker to secure the mesh in place over the hernia defect, ensuring it covers the surrounding tissue adequately.
The surgeon uses an absorbable polygalactane tacker to secure the mesh in place over the hernia defect, ensuring proper fixation.
The surgeon fires the absorbable tacker around the mesh, avoiding the areas known as the triangle of doom, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster.
The surgeon fires the tacker above the inguinal ligament, taking care to avoid the inferior epigastric vessel, which is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a supraumbilical port to position the absorbable tacker, firing at least ten centimeters above the upper border of the pubic symphysis and between the medial umbilical ligament. Care is taken to avoid firing below this area to prevent damage to the bladder, while the tacker is fixed onto the rectus abdominis.
The Y-Pro-II mesh is displayed, showing its inner layers made of proline and the outer weaving with Y-Trail material.
The mesh is visibly fixed all around the hernia repair site, indicating completion of the anchoring process.

The surgeon positions the mesh over the rectus abdominis muscle and secures it to the Cooper's ligament, ensuring fixation is done upon feeling the hardness of the ligament. Care is taken to avoid firing over the trapezoid of disaster, triangle of doom, and triangle of pain.
The surgeon secures the lower edge using a Dundee jamming knot, followed by continuous suturing and everting termination, ensuring the repair is stable and properly aligned.
The surgeon carefully pulls the mesh into position and fixes the inferomedial end of the mesh, ensuring proper placement within the surgical field.
The surgeon ties a starter knot using the Dundee jamming knot technique at the lower edge of the mesh during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon pulls the needle through the loop, automatically locking the suture in place as part of the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the application of a locking knot technique during the hernia repair procedure.

The surgeon stretches the peritoneum before taking a bite in the surgical field, being cautious of the potential presence of obturator vessels. The use of an endoskeletal approach is emphasized to avoid pricking the peritoneum.
The surgeon uses a needle to prick the peritoneum, preparing to take a bite in the surgical field.
The surgeon lifts the peritoneum and positions it near the tip of the needle, preparing to take a bite while ensuring careful handling to avoid pricking the peritoneum.
The surgeon continues to apply locking sutures to secure the tissue layers.
The surgeon ties a knot to lock the suture, ensuring the mesh is securely sealed inferiorly to prevent any bowel from lifting the mesh and entering the hernia defect.
The surgeon carefully lifts the peritoneum and pushes it over the needle, avoiding contact with the external iliac vein and artery.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the needle to avoid applying pressure on the peritoneum, ensuring it remains unpricked during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully lifts the peritoneum while gradually advancing the needle, ensuring gentle manipulation to avoid damage.
The surgeon navigates past the triangle of doom, an anatomical area bordered by the vas deferens and gonadal vessels, avoiding potential risks associated with this region during hernia repair.
The surgical field displays the spermatic vessel area, with visible anatomical structures relevant to the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon exercises caution while navigating near the spermatic vessel, which forms the lateral boundary of the triangle of doom during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon carefully manages a hematoma, ensuring not to prick it while continuing to lock the suture. After pulling the needle, the suture is tightened to secure the repair.
The surgeon adjusts the mesh, ensuring it is pressed firmly against the underlying tissue, likely the peritoneum, to secure its placement.

The surgeon carefully locks the sutures while manipulating the lower edge of the mesh, ensuring it is securely positioned without risk of injuring the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve (LFCN) or genitofemoral nerve (GFN).
The surgeon continues the process of continuous suturing, ensuring the stitches are evenly placed and secured along the surgical site.
The surgeon reinforces the entire myopectineal orifice, ensuring stability without the need for prepectineal tap or tap techniques.
The surgeon uses a large endoskeletal suture to interlock the tissue, rotating the suture to take a bite and secure a termination knot on the healthy tissue.

The surgeon demonstrates the technique for creating an abutment termination knot by forming a loop, passing the suture through it, and pulling the non-needle end to lock the knot. This process is repeated to secure the suture in place.
The surgeon pulls the non-needle end of the suture, causing it to lock again. At the end of the procedure, the needle is carefully extracted from the surgical site.
The surgeon observes the lower edge of the tissue, which is completely adhered to the mesh, highlighting the defect area in the hernia repair site.
The surgeon cuts the suture and inspects the area to ensure there is no possibility of omentum or bowel entering the repaired defect site.
